statement.markdown: Given the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. A cycle is when a node's next pointer points back to a previous node. To represent this, we will use a list of integers where each integer corresponds to a node's value and the next pointer is represented by the index of the next node in the list. If a node's next pointer points to `null`, it will be represented by `-1`. Your function should return `true` if a cycle is detected and `false` otherwise. For example, given the list `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` where `5` points back to `2`, the input would be `5` lines: the length of the list, followed by the values in the list, followed by the indices of the next pointers in the list. In this case, the input would be: `5`, `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`, `5`, `1`, `2`, `3`, `1`, `-1`. The output would be `true` because there is a cycle in the linked list.
statement.inputFormat: The first line will contain an integer `n`, the number of nodes in the linked list. The next `n` lines will contain the values of the nodes. The next `n` lines will contain the indices of the next pointers in the list. If a node's next pointer points to `null`, it will be represented by `-1`.
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing `true` if a cycle is detected in the linked list and `false` otherwise.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the linked list.
The values of the nodes in the linked list.
The indices of the next pointers in the linked list.

examples.explanation: There is a cycle in the linked list because the last node points back to the second node.
There is no cycle in the linked list because the last node points to null.

hints.text: Think about how you can use the given input to detect a cycle in the linked list.
Consider using Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm, also known as the "tortoise and the hare" algorithm, which uses two pointers moving at different speeds through the list.
One pointer should move one step at a time, while the other pointer should move two steps at a time. If there is a cycle, these two pointers will eventually meet.
To implement this, you can create two variables, one to keep track of the position of the first pointer and one to keep track of the position of the second pointer.
You can then use these two pointers to traverse the list, checking for a cycle by seeing if they ever meet.

editorial.title: Detecting Loops in Linked Lists
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by traversing the linked list and keeping track of visited nodes. A loop is detected when a node is revisited.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve traversing the linked list for each node, resulting in a time complexity of O(n^2). This is inefficient for large lists.
editorial.betterSolution: Using a set to store visited nodes allows for efficient lookups and insertions, reducing the time complexity to O(n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a set to keep track of visited nodes, achieving a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The solution is correct because it checks for the presence of a loop by verifying if a node's next pointer points to a previously visited node. If such a node is found, a loop is detected.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with a sample linked list, such as 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 2, demonstrates the solution's correctness. The set would contain nodes 1, 2, and 3. When the algorithm encounters the second occurrence of node 2, it detects a loop.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the linked list has a single node with a next pointer to itself.
Not checking for the presence of a loop when the linked list has multiple nodes with the same value.
Using a data structure with inefficient lookups, such as a list, to store visited nodes.
